content:
The ABI Boundary Problem

WebAssembly was designed to be a compilation target, not a runtime environment. The distinction matters when you're building for edge hardware where every millisecond of cold-start latency has a real cost. Before I profiled anything, I assumed the bottleneck was in the Wasm execution itself — the compilation from Rust to Wasm, the linear memory constraints, the lack of SIMD on ARM Cortex-A55. None of that was the problem.

The profiler showed 73% of total execution time at the Wasm/host boundary. The rendering logic was fast. The overhead of announcing each DOM operation to the host — one host call per node creation, per attribute set, per event listener registration — was the bottleneck. A typical Leptos component render on initial load makes several hundred of these crossings.

Every call from Wasm into the host crosses through the Wasm function table. This involves type checking, stack switching, and a context transition that costs roughly 50–200ns depending on the host. On its own, negligible. At hundreds of calls per render, it dominates.

The Ring Buffer Fix

The fix is batching. Instead of one host call per operation, the Wasm module accumulates operations in a 64-slot ring buffer in linear memory. When the buffer fills, or at a logical flush point at the end of a reactive update, a single flush_batch host call transfers the entire batch. The host reads directly from the Wasm linear memory pointer — no serialization, no copy, no intermediate allocation.

Why 64 slots

64 slots fits in a single cache line on ARM Cortex-A55 (64-byte cache line, 1-byte operation tags). A larger buffer reduces boundary crossings further but increases the latency of any single operation — you wait for the buffer to fill before the host sees it. 64 was the empirically derived sweet spot for the component sizes in this codebase. The result: ABI overhead dropped from 73% to under 3%. Same number of DOM operations, amortized across 64 per crossing instead of one.

AOT Compilation via Cranelift

The second major latency sink was JIT compilation at startup. The baseline runtime JIT-compiled the Wasm module on first load, contributing 9.6ms of the 11ms cold-start. Cranelift AOT compilation moves this to build time. The module is compiled to native ARM instructions once, cached alongside the .wasm binary, and loaded directly at startup — eliminating the JIT phase entirely.

Cold-start dropped from 11ms to 1.4ms. The tradeoff is a larger artifact cache and a build-time dependency on the target architecture — acceptable for a dedicated edge deployment, problematic for a multi-architecture distribution. For a fixed ARM Cortex-A55 target, it is a straightforward win.

Warm start: 180µs

After AOT, warm starts — where the compiled native artifact is already resident in the OS page cache — execute in 180µs. This is almost entirely the time to memory-map the artifact and call the Wasm start function. No compilation, no heap initialization beyond the bump allocator's initial sbrk. The latency distribution is tight: p50 and p99 are within 40µs of each other because there is no GC or JIT jitter in the critical path.

Deterministic Memory Layout

Rust's ownership model guarantees no heap allocation on the hot render path. All component state lives in Leptos signals backed by a bump allocator in a fixed linear memory region. The absence of a garbage collector means no stop-the-world pauses — and unlike a GC heap, the allocator's internal state is fully visible at compile time, which means the compiler can reason about aliasing and produce better machine code.

The Wasm linear memory model is both the runtime's greatest strength and its most surprising constraint. Pointers are 32-bit offsets into a flat address space — this makes host/guest memory sharing trivial (the host and guest share the same address space view) but means the entire application must fit in 4GB with no virtual memory separation between components. For a portfolio-scale SPA, this is not a constraint. For a multi-tenant edge runtime, it would be.

What the Profiler Actually Showed

The most useful thing I did was profile before optimizing. The pattern — the actual bottleneck being in the boundary between two systems rather than inside either one — is consistent across systems work. It shows up between Wasm and host, between kernel and userspace in eBPF pipelines, between network client and server in distributed tracing. The fix is always to batch across the boundary. Measure first, then batch.

01

Profile at the boundary layer, not just inside each component. Boundaries between systems — Wasm/host, kernel/userspace, network hops — are invisible to application-level profiling and account for a disproportionate share of latency in high-call-rate paths.

02

wasm-opt's --enable-bulk-memory flag generates instructions that the bundled wasm-opt in older Trunk versions cannot parse. Pin wasm-opt = "0.116.1" in Trunk.toml. This is a non-obvious toolchain compatibility issue that cost several hours and produces no helpful error message.

03

Leptos 0.6 CSR reactive signals update only the DOM nodes affected by a signal change — not the entire component tree. This is the key architectural difference from React's virtual DOM diffing and the reason Leptos achieves lower update latency at the cost of more explicit dependency declaration.

04

AOT compilation is architecture-specific. A binary compiled for ARM Cortex-A55 will not run on x86_64. For a multi-target edge deployment, you need either per-architecture build artifacts or a JIT fallback — the performance benefit of AOT does not come free.
